title: Learning Reactive Synthesis from Model Checking Feedback

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach AI to write bug-free code by first letting it copy humans and then having a digital 'judge' yell at it whenever it made a mistake. They claim this makes the AI smarter and its code leaner, theoretically solving puzzles that even the smartest traditional tools find impossible.
why_bad: The 'hybrid approach' is basically just standard pre-training followed by reinforcement learning, making the 'novelty' about as fresh as three-day-old sushi.
Claiming 'state-of-the-art' results on toy problems is like winning a gold medal in a 5-meter dash—it's technically a win, but nobody is impressed.

The paper glosses over the massive computational cost of running a formal model checker inside a training loop, which is a bit like saying your car is fast while ignoring that it consumes three gallons of gas per mile.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you want to become a world-class singer. First, you spend weeks mimicking Taylor Swift records (supervised learning) until you sound okay, but you don't actually understand music theory. To improve, you go to a karaoke bar where the judge is a literal robot that only cares if you hit the exact right frequencies (model checking). If the robot doesn't buzz you off stage, you keep doing exactly that. The authors are proud because they finally hit a high C, but the reviewers rejected it because everyone already knows that 'practicing with a tuner' works, and they're skeptical that this singer could handle anything more complex than 'Twinkle Twinkle Little Star' without the robot's constant screaming.

title: PLAGUE: Plug-and-play Framework for Lifelong Adaptive Generation of Multi-turn Exploits

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of asking an AI to do something bad directly, this system uses a multi-step 'gaslighting' technique to slowly trick the model into breaking its own rules over a long conversation. It basically turns jailbreaking into a three-act play where the AI is coached into being a villain before it even realizes what's happening.
why_bad: The 'lifelong learning' claim is a massive stretch; the system is essentially just storing chat logs and retrying prompts, which is basic session memory, not a breakthrough in neuro-symbolic evolution.

Naming your framework 'PLAGUE' and demonstrating how to bypass safety on unreleased or cutting-edge models without offering equivalent defensive solutions is an ethical red flag that scares away reviewers from 'High Stakes' tracks.

The 'state-of-the-art' results likely suffer from 'over-optimization' on a small set of prompts; if you poke a model 100 times until it breaks, you haven't found a systemic flaw, you've just found the model's patience threshold.
roast.analogy_name: The Long Con at the Library
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you want to check out a restricted book on 'How to Build a Trebuchet' from a high-security library. A 'single-turn' attack is walking up and screaming 'GIVE ME THE SIEGE MANUAL,' which gets you banned immediately. PLAGUE is like showing up at the library three days in a row: on Monday, you ask where the history section is (Primer); on Tuesday, you chat with the librarian about medieval architecture and buy them a coffee (Planner); and on Wednesday, you convince them that you’re a certified restorer who needs the trebuchet book to 'save the spine' (Finisher). You aren't a scientist; you're just a really persistent grifter who found a way to make the librarian feel awkward enough to say yes.

title: Brain-Informed Language Model Training Enables Scalable and Generalizable Alignment with Human Brain Activity

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to make AI smarter by feeding it brain scans of people binge-watching 'Friends,' hoping the drama between Ross and Rachel would teach the model how humans actually perceive the world. They found that tethering a chatbot to literal gray matter helps it understand visual concepts better than text alone, though it’s essentially learning through high-tech eavesdropping on our thoughts.
why_bad: Underpowered Sample Size: Relying on only six people's brain data to represent 'humanity' is statistically equivalent to assuming everyone in the world thinks exactly like a small group of people who still enjoy 90s sitcoms.

The 'Correlation vs. Causation' Trap: The reviewers likely noted that just because the model can mimic brain voxels doesn't mean it's actually 'thinking' like a human—it might just be a very expensive exercise in pattern-matching noise.
Marginal Utility: The performance boost on commonsense tasks is negligible compared to the massive computational cost and the sheer logistical nightmare of sticking people in fMRI tubes for 50 hours.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine trying to teach a blind person what 'red' looks like by having them hold the hand of a stranger who is watching a sunset. You’re hoping the stranger's sweaty palms and elevated heart rate somehow translate into an understanding of light waves. This paper argues that if the stranger watches enough TV, the blind person will eventually become a master painter. In reality, you've just created a very expensive way to track someone's pulse while they're distracted by Jennifer Aniston’s haircut.

title: TextME: Text-only Training for Modality Expansion via LLM Space Pivoting

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach an AI to understand specialized data like X-rays and molecules using only text descriptions, skipping the expensive process of showing it actual pairs of images and labels. They claimed that because different types of data share a similar 'mathematical geometry,' the AI could magically figure out what a 3D model looks like just by reading a Wikipedia entry about it.
why_bad: The core assumption—that 'modality gaps' are consistent across totally different sensors—is a massive leap of faith that ignores how messy real-world data geometry actually is.
The '91.5% of paired-data performance' claim is suspiciously high, likely because the benchmarks used were so trivial that even a broken clock (or a poorly aligned model) could look like a genius.

Reviewers noted that the 'emergent capabilities' like audio-to-3D are likely just the model memorizing text labels rather than actually understanding the relationship between sound waves and spatial coordinates.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine hiring an interior designer who has been blind since birth and has only ever read books about colors. They argue that because the word 'Navy' is exactly five inches away from the word 'Azure' in their dictionary, they can perfectly coordinate your living room paint without ever actually seeing a swatch. TextME is basically trying to renovate a mansion (your multimodal model) using nothing but a ruler and a thesaurus, then acting surprised when the 'audio-to-3D' feature accidentally paints your kitchen with the sound of a lawnmower.

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a specialized dashboard to track exactly how much electricity AI models guzzle and how much carbon they spit out. They officially confirmed that bigger models are power hogs and that running AI on a standard computer processor instead of a graphics card is a great way to waste both time and energy.
why_bad: Incremental Contribution: The paper largely 'discovers' things we've known for years, such as GPUs being more efficient than CPUs and reduced precision (BF16) saving energy.
Methodological Redundancy: Critics noted that the tool is essentially a wrapper for existing libraries like CodeCarbon, offering a new leaderboard but no fundamentally new way to measure power.

Predictor Overkill: Using complex Gradient Boosting models to predict energy usage was seen as unnecessary fluff, as simple linear scaling usually gets you 90% of the way there without the extra math.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine writing a 10-page academic paper announcing a 'revolutionary' device that plugs into your toaster to measure its electricity usage. You conclude that toasting a giant bagel takes more power than a thin slice of white bread, and that a modern toaster is more efficient than holding your bread over a scented candle. While the data is technically accurate and the leaderboard is shiny, the 'breakthrough' feels a bit flat because everyone already knew that heat costs money and candles are a terrible way to make breakfast.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically grounded snark delivered with 99.9% precision.
